article.title: Postmenopausal Estrogen Therapy and Cardiovascular Disease: Ten-Year Follow-up from the Nurses' Health Study
article.authors: Stampfer MJ, Colditz GA, Willett WC, Manson JE, Rosner B, Speizer FE, Hennekels CH

study_type.notes: Prospective cohort study. Classic Nurses' Health Study. 48,470 postmenopausal women aged 30-63, free of cancer and CVD at baseline. 10-year follow-up (337,854 person-years). Evaluated estrogen therapy effect on cardiovascular disease risk.

1.notes: Title clearly indicates study design: 'Ten-Year Follow-up from the Nurses' Health Study' indicates prospective cohort

2.notes: Background: 'The effect of postmenopausal estrogen therapy on the risk of cardiovascular disease remains controversial...existing evidence conflicting'

3.notes: Objectives: 'We followed...postmenopausal women...to evaluate effect of estrogen therapy on stroke and coronary disease risk'

4.notes: Study design: clearly presented as prospective cohort study in Methods section

5.notes: Setting and dates: participants in the Nurses' Health Study (1976 baseline); follow-up through 1990; 48,470 women enrolled at baseline; up to 10 years of follow-up

6a.notes: Eligibility criteria: postmenopausal women aged 30-63; without history of cancer or cardiovascular disease at baseline; method of selection from NHS cohort clearly described

7.notes: Outcomes defined: major coronary disease (nonfatal MI or coronary death), stroke, total mortality, cardiovascular mortality. Exposure: estrogen therapy (current use, former use, never use)

8.notes: Data sources: biennial questionnaires for exposure and covariate data; medical record review and death certificates for outcome validation; standardized assessment methods

9.notes: Efforts to address bias: validation of reported events with medical records; sensitivity analyses excluding women with recent physician visits; analyses in low-risk subgroups

10.notes: No explicit sample size calculation described - used all eligible cohort members

11.notes: Minimal discussion of quantitative variable handling; main analysis uses exposure categories (current, former, never users)

12a.notes: Statistical methods: Cox proportional hazards regression for adjustment of confounders; relative risk estimates with 95% CI; stratified analyses by menopausal type

12b.notes: Subgroup analyses: by type of menopause (natural vs surgical); by smoking status and other risk factors; stratified analysis results presented

12c.notes: No explicit discussion of missing data handling

12d.notes: Loss to follow-up methods: biennial questionnaire response rates tracked; 88% response rate maintained through follow-up period

12e.notes: Sensitivity analyses: analyses restricted to women with recent physician visits; analyses in low-risk subgroups excluding smokers, diabetics, hypertensive, hypercholesterolemic

13a.notes: Participant flow: 48,470 women at baseline free of cancer and CVD; follow-up through 10 years; 337,854 person-years documented

13b.notes: No explicit discussion of reasons for non-participation; cohort members tracked through questionnaire returns

14a.notes: Baseline characteristics by estrogen use status: age, smoking, diabetes, hypertension, cholesterol levels, BMI presented in table format

14b.notes: No explicit reporting of missing data completeness

15.notes: Outcome events: 224 strokes, 405 major coronary events, 1,263 deaths from all causes documented over follow-up period

16a.notes: Main findings with precision: adjusted RR for current estrogen use = 0.56 (95% CI 0.40-0.80); for all causes mortality RR=0.89 (CI 0.78-1.00); for CVD mortality RR=0.72 (CI 0.55-0.95)

16b.notes: Main analysis uses categorical exposure groups (current, former, never); no continuous variable categorization issues

16c.notes: Relative risk estimates provided but not translated to absolute risk or number needed to treat

17.notes: Other analyses: subgroup analyses by menopausal type, smoking status; sensitivity analyses in different population subsets with varying inclusion criteria

18.notes: Key results: 'current use of estrogen reduced risk of major coronary disease by 44%; risk reduction significant for both natural and surgical menopause'

19.notes: No explicit discussion of study limitations or potential biases in Discussion section

20.notes: Overall interpretation: findings suggest estrogen therapy protective for coronary disease; results consistent with prior 4-year follow-up data but conflict with Framingham concurrent report

21.notes: Limited discussion of generalizability; cohort of nurses may limit external validity

22.notes: Funding: National Institutes of Health, NIH Public Health Service grants cited in funding statement
